lines.text: APPENDIX
A few topics that didn't fit neatly into the main lecture flow but are worth knowing.
The "CCS" versus "HiFi" naming.
These two terms are used somewhat interchangeably.
CCS — circular consensus sequencing — refers to the underlying algorithm (averaging across multiple passes of the same SMRTbell).
HiFi refers to the product (reads with Q20-plus accuracy generated by CCS).
In practice, HiFi has become the dominant marketing term, but if you read older papers or technical documentation, CCS often appears.
They refer to essentially the same thing in modern usage.
PacBio sometimes distinguishes "CCS reads" (any consensus from multiple passes) from "HiFi reads" (CCS reads passing the Q20 quality filter), but for most purposes you can treat them as the same.
Sample input requirements.

PacBio HiFi typically requires microgram-quantities of input DNA, and the DNA needs to be high-molecular-weight (typically tens to hundreds of kilobases of average size in the input) to support the fifteen-to-twenty-kilobase library prep.
This is a meaningful constraint — for samples with limited or fragmented DNA, PacBio HiFi may not be feasible without amplification (which defeats the methylation calling) or pooling.

PacBio has been working on lower-input workflows, and the current Revio chemistry can run from lower inputs than older platforms, but the high-molecular-weight requirement remains a practical consideration.
Library prep options.

Beyond the standard HiFi workflow with size-selected inserts around 15-20 kb, PacBio supports several specialized library types: Iso-Seq for transcripts, MAS-Seq for ultra-multiplexed single-cell transcript reads, ultralong libraries for spanning extreme repeats, targeted enrichment libraries for specific genomic regions, and amplicon libraries for studying short, defined regions in high depth.
Each has slightly different workflows but all use the SMRTbell architecture.
hifiasm and modern assemblers.
The assembler hifiasm, developed by Heng Li's group, has become the de facto standard tool for assembling genomes from HiFi data.
It handles diploid (phased) assembly natively, producing two haplotype-resolved contigs per region of heterozygosity, and it scales to large genomes efficiently.
Other long-read assemblers like Flye and Canu also work with HiFi data.
The contiguity routinely achieved by these assemblers — contigs spanning entire chromosome arms in many cases — would have been unimaginable in the short-read era.
The pangenome era.

As HiFi assemblies become routine for diverse human samples and for many species, the field is shifting from "single reference genome" thinking to "pangenome" thinking — representing the genetic diversity of a species as a graph or collection of assemblies rather than a single linear sequence.
The Human Pangenome Reference Consortium, building on the T2T work, is constructing the first true human pangenome.
PacBio HiFi is the underlying technology making this practical, because reference-quality assemblies are now affordable and reproducible.
Methylation calling tools.
PacBio's official tools for methylation calling include the primrose and jasmine software packages, which call 5mC from HiFi kinetics.
Open-source tools like pb-CpG-tools also exist.
The Nanopore community has its own ecosystem (megalodon, modkit, and others).
The tools are evolving rapidly; if you're considering methylation work, check the current state-of-the-art before designing your experiment.
Clinical and regulatory status.
As of the early-2020s, PacBio Revio has received CE-IVD marking in Europe for clinical use, and PacBio is pursuing FDA pathways for clinical applications in the US.

Several clinical labs have adopted HiFi for rare disease work, and a number of published case reports describe HiFi-enabled diagnoses in patients who had remained undiagnosed with Illumina-only workups.
The clinical footprint is growing but still small compared to Illumina; that gap is expected to narrow over the next several years.
A note on Sanger sequencing and where it still fits.

Sanger sequencing, the original sequencing method from the 1970s, is still in use today for validating individual variants, sequencing small numbers of plasmid clones, and confirming results from larger experiments.
Sanger reads are about a kilobase long, very accurate, and inexpensive on a per-sample basis.

HiFi has not replaced Sanger for these targeted applications; HiFi competes with Illumina for whole-genome and whole-transcriptome work, and with Nanopore for long-read applications, but the ecological niche of Sanger — single-sample, targeted, gold-standard validation — remains intact.
A note on the politics of long-read sequencing.

The PacBio-Nanopore rivalry has been intense, often crossing into public marketing battles, with each company strenuously emphasizing the strengths of its own platform and the weaknesses of the other.

As a student or working scientist, you should evaluate platforms by application rather than by marketing — the right tool depends on what you're trying to do, and both platforms have legitimate strengths for specific tasks.
Pay attention to peer-reviewed benchmark studies rather than to vendor materials, especially when the question is which platform to use for a new project.
Recommended reading and resources.

For the underlying technology, the original 2009 Science paper by Eid et al. introducing single-molecule real-time sequencing is the canonical reference, and Korlach's papers on ZMW physics are accessible.
For HiFi and CCS specifically, the 2019 paper by Wenger et al. on accurate circular consensus sequencing is the entry point.
For T2T human genome work, the 2022 Science papers from the consortium are mandatory reading.
For Iso-Seq, see the various reviews from PacBio and the Tilgner group.
For comparison with Nanopore, look at recent benchmark studies from independent academic groups — the Genome in a Bottle consortium has done useful work here.
Final summary.

PacBio HiFi sequencing is single-molecule, real-time, optical sequencing on chips with millions of zero-mode waveguides, using engineered phi29 polymerase and phospholinked fluorescent nucleotides, with circular SMRTbell templates enabling multiple-pass reading of the same molecule and consensus-based error correction.

The result is long-and-accurate reads — fifteen to twenty kilobases at Q30-plus quality — that have transformed genome assembly, structural variant calling, full-length transcriptomics, and direct methylation analysis.
The platform completed the human genome, drives the modern pangenome era, and is steadily moving into clinical genomics.
Its rivalry with Nanopore defines the modern long-read field, with PacBio as the precision tool and Nanopore as the length-and-flexibility champion.
After fifteen years of difficult execution, the platform has fulfilled its founding promise.
The accuracy problem is solved.
Alright.
That's the end of the formal lecture.
Thanks for your attention through what is, I know, a dense couple of hours.
I'll take questions for the next twenty minutes or so.
Anyone want to start?
